From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Britishness](/w/index.php?title=Britishness&redirect=no "Britishness"))

State or quality of embodying British characteristics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/ae/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg)

The [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack"), in addition to being the flag of the United Kingdom, also serves as one of the most potent symbols of Britishness.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECommission_for_Racial_Equality200521-1)

**British national identity** is a term referring to the sense of [national identity](/wiki/National_identity "National identity"), as embodied in the shared and characteristic [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom"), [languages](/wiki/Languages_of_the_United_Kingdom "Languages of the United Kingdom") and [traditions](/wiki/Category:British_traditions "Category:British traditions"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) of the [British people](/wiki/British_people "British people").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-oed-4) It comprises the claimed qualities that bind and distinguish the [British people](/wiki/British_people "British people") and form the basis of their unity and identity,[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWrightGamble200932-5) and the expressions of [British culture](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom")—such as habits, behaviours, or symbols—that have a common, familiar or iconic quality readily identifiable with the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). Dialogue about the legitimacy and authenticity of Britishness is intrinsically tied with power relations and politics;[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECommission_for_Racial_Equality200511–12-6) in terms of [nationhood](/wiki/Nationhood "Nationhood") and belonging, expressing or recognising one's Britishness provokes a range of responses and attitudes, such as advocacy, indifference, or rejection.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECommission_for_Racial_Equality200511–12-6)

Although the term 'Britishness' "[sprang] into political and academic prominence" only in the late 20th century,[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWrightGamble2009149-7) its origins lie with the formation of the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") in 1707. It was used with reference to Britons collectively as early as 1682,[[4]](#cite_note-oed-4) and the historian [Linda Colley](/wiki/Linda_Colley "Linda Colley") asserts that it was after the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") that the ethnic groups of Great Britain began to assume a "layered" identity—to think of themselves as simultaneously British but also [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people"), [English](/wiki/English_people "English people"), and/or [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColley199212–13-8) In this formative period, Britishness was "closely bound up with [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism")".[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColley19928-9) The *Oxford English Dictionary Online* dates the first known use of the term Britishness to refer to the state of being British to a June 1857 issue of *[Putnam's Monthly Magazine](/wiki/Putnam%27s_Monthly_Magazine "Putnam's Monthly Magazine")*.[[4]](#cite_note-oed-4)

Since the late 20th century, the exploration and proliferation of Britishness became directly associated with a desire to define, sustain or restore a homogeneous British identity or allegiance to Britain, prompting debate. For instance, the [Life in the United Kingdom test](/wiki/Life_in_the_United_Kingdom_test "Life in the United Kingdom test")—reported as a test of one's Britishness—has been described as controversial.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The [UK Independence Party](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party") have asserted that Britishness is tied with inclusive [civic nationalism](/wiki/Civic_nationalism "Civic nationalism"),[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) whereas the [Commission for Racial Equality](/wiki/Commission_for_Racial_Equality "Commission for Racial Equality") reported that Scots, Welsh, Irish and [ethnic minorities](/wiki/Ethnic_minorities "Ethnic minorities") may feel quite divorced from Britishness because of ethnic English dominance; [Gwynfor Evans](/wiki/Gwynfor_Evans "Gwynfor Evans"), a [Welsh nationalist](/wiki/Welsh_nationalism "Welsh nationalism") politician, said that "Britishness is a political synonym for Englishness which extends English culture over the Scots, Welsh, and the Irish."[[13]](#cite_note-13) Historians Graham Macphee and Prem Poddar state that Britishness and Englishness are invariably conflated as they are both tied to the identity of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") and UK; slippage between the two words is common.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
With regard to a proposed oath of allegiance for school leavers, historian [David Starkey](/wiki/David_Starkey "David Starkey") argued that it is impossible to teach Britishness because "a British nation doesn't exist".[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Government perspective

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_national_identity&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Government perspective")]

[Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown"), then [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer"), made a speech in 2006 to promote the idea of Britishness.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Brown's speech to the [Fabian Society](/wiki/Fabian_Society "Fabian Society")'s Britishness Conference proposed that **British values** demand a new constitutional settlement and symbols to represent a modern patriotism, including a new youth community service scheme and a '[British Day](/wiki/British_Day "British Day")' to celebrate.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

One of the central issues identified at the Fabian Society conference was how the English identity fits within the framework of a devolved UK. Does England require a new constitutional settlement for instance?[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The [British government](/wiki/British_government "British government") has sought to promote Britishness with the inaugural [Veterans' Day](/wiki/Armed_Forces_Day_(United_Kingdom) "Armed Forces Day (United Kingdom)") (now called [Armed Forces Day](/wiki/Armed_Forces_Day_(United_Kingdom) "Armed Forces Day (United Kingdom)")), first held on 27 June 2006. As well as celebrating the achievements of members of the armed forces, at the first event for the celebration Brown said:

> Scots and people from the rest of the UK share the purpose —that Britain has something to say to the rest of the world about the values of freedom, democracy, and the dignity of the people that you stand up for. So at a time when people can talk about football and devolution and money, it is important that we also remember the values that we share in common.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Critics have argued that Brown's sudden interest in the subject had more to do with countering English opposition to a [Scottish](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") Member of Parliament becoming prime minister.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In November 2007, *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* newspaper's *Comment Central* asked readers to define Britishness in five little words. The winning suggestion was "No motto please, we're British".[[21]](#cite_note-Hurst-21)

A duty to promote [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") forms a key part of the "duty to actively promote fundamental British values in schools" in the United Kingdom in accordance with section 78 of the [Education Act 2002](/wiki/Education_Act_2002 "Education Act 2002"). According to the [Department for Education](/wiki/Department_for_Education "Department for Education")'s advice for maintained schools in 2014, "Schools should promote the fundamental British values of democracy, the rule of law, individual liberty, and mutual respect and tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs".[[22]](#cite_note-DfE2014-22) The Government's [*Prevent* strategy](/wiki/CONTEST "CONTEST") of 2011 was cited as the source of this list of values, but that strategy also contained a slightly different list: "democracy, rule of law, equality of opportunity, freedom of speech and the rights of all men and women to live free from persecution of any kind."[[23]](#cite_note-23) The 2018 version of the [CONTEST](/wiki/CONTEST "CONTEST") strategy codified the list as:

* the rule of law
* individual liberty
* democracy
* mutual respect, tolerance and understanding of different faiths and beliefs.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The same advice stated that UK schools must:

* encourage respect for democracy and support for participation in the democratic processes
* [ensure pupils acquire] an understanding of how citizens can influence decision-making through the democratic process

for example by

* [including] in suitable parts of the curriculum, as appropriate for the age of pupils, material on the strengths, advantages and disadvantages of democracy, and how democracy and the law works in Britain, in contrast to other forms of government in other countries;
* [ensuring] that all pupils within the school have a voice that is listened to, and demonstrate how democracy works by actively promoting democratic processes, such as a school council whose members are voted for by the pupils.[[22]](#cite_note-DfE2014-22)

After the spread of the [COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom") in 2020, Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") delivered a [special address](/wiki/List_of_addresses_to_the_nation_by_the_British_monarch "List of addresses to the nation by the British monarch") that listed "the attributes of self-discipline, of quiet good-humoured resolve and of fellow-feeling" as characteristic of Britain.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Ethnicity and social trends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_national_identity&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ethnicity and social trends")]

Due to immigration from other countries, not all people residing in England and the United Kingdom are [White](/wiki/White_people "White people"). According to the [2011 census](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_census "2011 United Kingdom census") in England, around 85.4% of residents are White (British, Irish, other European), 7.8% Asian (mainly [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia")), 3.5% Black, 2.3% are of [mixed-race](/wiki/Mixed-race "Mixed-race") heritage, 0.4% Arab, and 0.6% identified as Other ethnicity, with a significantly higher non-white population in large cities such as [London](/wiki/London#Ethnic_groups "London").

A survey conducted in 2007 found that the majority of people in many non-white ethnic minority groups living in Great Britain described their national identity as British, English, Scottish or Welsh. This included almost nine in ten (87%) of people with [mixed heritage](/wiki/British_Mixed-Race "British Mixed-Race"), 85% of [Black Caribbean people](/wiki/British_African-Caribbean_community "British African-Caribbean community"), 80% of [Pakistanis](/wiki/British_Pakistanis "British Pakistanis") and 78% of [Bangladeshis](/wiki/British_Bangladeshi "British Bangladeshi"). Non-whites were more likely to describe themselves as British than whites. One-third of people from the [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British") group described themselves as British; the remaining two-thirds of respondents identified themselves as [English](/wiki/English_people "English people"), Welsh, or Scottish ethnic groups.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

A study conducted for the [Commission for Racial Equality](/wiki/Commission_for_Racial_Equality "Commission for Racial Equality") (CRE) in 2005 found that, in England, the majority of ethnic minority participants identified primarily as British, whereas ethnic English participants identified as English first and British second. In [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), the majority of both white and ethnic minority participants identified as Welsh or Scottish first and British second, although they saw no incompatibility between the two identities.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECommission_for_Racial_Equality200537-27) Other research conducted for the CRE found that white participants felt that there was a threat to Britishness from large-scale immigration, the "unfair" claims that they perceived ethnic minorities made on the [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state"), a rise in [moral pluralism](/wiki/Value_pluralism "Value pluralism"), and [political correctness](/wiki/Political_correctness "Political correctness"). Much of this frustration was found to be targeted at [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") rather than minorities in general. Muslim participants in the study reported feeling victimised and stated that they felt that they were being asked to choose between Muslim and British identities, whereas they saw it possible to be both at the same time.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECommission_for_Racial_Equality20054-28)

## Within the United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_national_identity&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Within the United Kingdom")]

### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_national_identity&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: England")]

See also: [English national identity](/wiki/English_national_identity "English national identity")

### Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_national_identity&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Scotland")]

See also: [Scottish national identity](/wiki/Scottish_national_identity "Scottish national identity")

National Identity in Scotland from 1997 to 2003 (in %)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

| Identity | 1997 | 1999 | 2001 | 2003 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Scottish not British | 23 | 32 | 36 | 31 |
| More Scottish than British | 38 | 35 | 30 | 34 |
| Equally Scottish and British | 27 | 22 | 24 | 22 |
| More British than Scottish | 4 | 3 | 3 | 4 |
| British not Scottish | 4 | 4 | 3 | 4 |

There is evidence that [people in Scotland](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") are increasingly likely to describe themselves as Scottish, and less likely to say they are British. A 2006 study by social scientists at the Universities of Edinburgh, Dundee, St Andrews and Lancaster shows that more than eight out of ten people in Scotland saw themselves as Scottish. At the same time, there has been a long-term decline in Scots defining themselves as British, although more than half of the people in the survey saw themselves as British.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In the [2011 Census](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Census_2011 "United Kingdom Census 2011") in Scotland:[[32]](#cite_note-nationalidentity-32)

* 62% identified themselves as **Scottish only**
* 18% identified themselves as **Scottish and British**
* 8% identified themselves as **British only**

In the [2021 Census](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Census_2021 "United Kingdom Census 2021") in Scotland:[[33]](#cite_note-33)

* 65.5% identified themselves as **Scottish only**
* 8.2% identified themselves as **Scottish and British**
* 13.9% identified themselves as **British only**

The [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") [MSP](/wiki/Member_of_the_Scottish_Parliament "Member of the Scottish Parliament") and [Cabinet Secretary for Justice](/wiki/Cabinet_Secretary_for_Justice "Cabinet Secretary for Justice"), [Kenny MacAskill](/wiki/Kenny_MacAskill "Kenny MacAskill") gave the following submission to the [UK Parliament](/wiki/UK_Parliament "UK Parliament")'s Joint Committee on Human Rights in March 2008 discussing a [British Bill of Rights](/wiki/Proposed_British_Bill_of_Rights "Proposed British Bill of Rights"):

> What is meant by Britishness? Is there a concept of Britishness? Yes, just as there is a concept of being Scandinavian. We eat fish and chips, we eat chicken masala, we watch EastEnders. Are [the SNP] British? No, we are not. We consider ourselves Scottish.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_national_identity&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Wales")]

Similar to Scotland, results from the Annual Population Survey (APS) conducted by the [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics"), show that the majority of people residing in Wales describe themselves as [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people").[[35]](#cite_note-auto-35) Respondents were asked whether they considered their national identity to be 'Welsh', or 'Non-Welsh' (defined as: 'English', 'Scottish', 'Irish', 'British' or 'Other'). In June 2017, 63.2% of respondents in Wales defined their national identity as 'Welsh'.[[35]](#cite_note-auto-35)

### Identity and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_national_identity&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Identity and politics")]

In a 1998 poll, 37% of [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") voters stated themselves to be "Scottish, not British", the rest demonstrating some form of British identity, with the most popular choice being "More Scottish than British" (41%).[[36]](#cite_note-36) This conclusion was again put forward in 2002, with similar figures cited.[[37]](#cite_note-37) However, the British Social Attitudes Survey of 2007 found that only 21% of Scots saw themselves as 'Equally Scottish and British', with less than half choosing British as a secondary identity.[[38]](#cite_note-BSA2007-38) The report concluded that 73% of respondents saw themselves as 'only' or 'mainly' Scottish.[[38]](#cite_note-BSA2007-38)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_national_identity&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: References")]
